title: Business Writing with AI
description: Master the art of using Claude for professional writing — from emails and proposals to presentations and internal communications.
learningObjectives: Craft prompts that produce business writing matching your organization's voice, style, and level of formality
Use Claude to draft, edit, and refine professional emails, proposals, and presentations with targeted follow-up prompts
Adapt Claude's writing output for different business contexts — from casual Slack updates to formal board communications

sections.title: Crafting Effective Prompts for Business Writing
sections.content:
Professional writing is one of Claude's strongest use cases — but the gap between a mediocre AI-drafted email and one that sounds genuinely human comes down to how you prompt. Generic prompts produce generic writing. Specific, context-rich prompts produce writing that sounds like you.

**The Five Elements of a Business Writing Prompt**

Every business writing prompt should include these elements, in roughly this order:

• **Role and voice.** Tell Claude who it's writing as and what tone to use. "Write as a senior account manager" produces different prose than "write as a junior team member." Include specific voice characteristics: "professional but warm," "direct and concise," or "formal and measured."
• **Audience and relationship.** Who's receiving this? What's your relationship with them? "Write to a long-term client we've worked with for three years" produces a very different email than "write to a prospect we've never spoken to." The relationship context shapes formality, assumed knowledge, and tone.
• **Purpose and desired outcome.** What should this writing accomplish? "Inform them of the delay" is fine, but "inform them of the delay and maintain their confidence in our team" gives Claude the subtext it needs to strike the right note.
• **Key points to include.** List the specific facts, arguments, or requests that must appear in the output. Don't make Claude guess what you want to say — provide the substance and let Claude shape the delivery.
• **Constraints.** Length limits, formatting requirements, things to avoid. "Keep this under 200 words," "don't use the word 'synergy,'" or "avoid technical jargon — the reader is non-technical" are all useful constraints.

**Before and After: Prompt Quality Matters**

A weak prompt: "Write an email to a client about a project delay."

A strong prompt: "Write a 150-word email from me (Senior PM at a consulting firm) to Sarah Chen, our primary contact at Meridian Corp. We've worked together for two years and have a good relationship. The data migration phase of their CRM project will be delayed by two weeks due to unexpected data quality issues in their legacy system. The new completion date is March 28. I want to communicate this transparently while emphasizing that we caught the issue early and have a remediation plan. Tone should be professional, direct, and reassuring — not apologetic or defensive."

The strong prompt produces writing that sounds like a real person in a real situation. The weak prompt produces a template that could have come from anywhere.

**Building a Prompt Library**

If you write the same types of communications regularly, build a library of prompt templates. A "client update email" template, a "project status report" template, and a "meeting recap" template will save you time and ensure consistency. Store them in a document and customize the specifics for each use.

sections.title: Email Drafting and Editing
sections.content:
Email is the most common business writing task, and it's where Claude delivers the most immediate time savings. But "write me an email" is the beginning of the process, not the end. The real skill is using Claude as a collaborative editor that helps you refine the message until it's exactly right.

**Drafting Emails with Claude**

For a first draft, give Claude the substance and let it handle the structure and polish:

• **Provide the raw content.** Dump your thoughts in bullet points, stream-of-consciousness notes, or even a voice transcript. Claude can turn messy inputs into clean prose. "Here's what I need to say: we're launching the new feature next Tuesday, the team worked really hard on it, there are a few known issues we're monitoring, and I need everyone to test it before the client demo on Thursday" becomes a well-structured announcement email.
• **Specify the email type.** Different emails have different conventions. An introduction email has a different rhythm than a follow-up. A request for information is structured differently than a thank-you note. Naming the type helps Claude apply the right conventions.
• **Include the desired action.** What should the recipient do after reading? Reply with feedback? Approve a budget? Attend a meeting? The call to action shapes the entire email — where Claude places emphasis, how it structures the argument, and how it closes.

**Editing and Refining**

Claude is often more useful as an editor than a drafter. If you've already written something, use Claude to improve it:

• **Tone adjustment.** "Make this more diplomatic" or "this needs to be more direct — the reader keeps missing my point" are both common refinement requests that Claude handles well.
• **Concision.** "This email is too long. Cut it by half while keeping all the key points." Claude is good at identifying which sentences are doing real work and which are padding.
• **Clarity check.** "Read this email as if you're the recipient. What's confusing or ambiguous?" Using Claude as a reader reveals blind spots in your writing.
• **Sensitivity review.** For difficult emails — performance feedback, contract negotiations, bad news — ask Claude: "Review this email for anything that could be misinterpreted or cause offense." A second perspective on sensitive communications is always valuable.

**Email Sequences and Threading**

For complex conversations that span multiple emails, give Claude the context of the thread:

• Paste the previous emails in the conversation so Claude understands what's already been said.
• Indicate what changed since the last email and what new information you need to convey.
• Ask Claude to maintain consistency with the tone and commitments in earlier messages.

sections.title: Proposal and Pitch Creation
sections.content:
Proposals and pitches are higher-stakes writing that needs to persuade, not just inform. Claude can help structure compelling arguments, but the strategic thinking — which angles to emphasize, which objections to preempt, which evidence to lead with — should come from you.

**Structuring a Proposal with Claude**

A strong proposal follows a logical arc that moves the reader from problem recognition to solution acceptance:

• **Problem statement.** Start with the pain. Claude can help you articulate the problem in terms the reader cares about, but you need to provide the specific pain points and stakes. Tell Claude: "The client's main concerns are X, Y, and Z. Frame the problem statement around these priorities."
• **Proposed solution.** Describe what you're recommending and why it addresses the stated problem. Ask Claude to connect each element of your solution back to a specific problem point. This cause-and-effect structure makes the proposal more convincing.
• **Evidence and credibility.** Case studies, data points, testimonials, or past results. Give Claude the raw evidence and ask it to weave it into the narrative: "Incorporate these three case studies to support the ROI claims."
• **Implementation plan.** Timeline, milestones, responsibilities. Claude can format this into a clean table or Gantt-style overview if you provide the raw timeline data.
• **Investment and ROI.** Frame pricing as an investment, not a cost. Ask Claude to build the value argument before presenting the numbers.

**Pitch Decks and Slide Narratives**

Claude can't create slides directly, but it's excellent at writing the narrative that goes on them:

• **One idea per slide.** Ask Claude to break your pitch into discrete points, each expressed in one headline sentence and 2-3 supporting bullets. This translates directly to slide content.
• **Speaker notes.** For each slide, ask Claude to write the talking points you'd use when presenting. These should be conversational, not a repetition of what's on the slide.
• **Narrative flow.** Ask Claude to sequence your points for maximum impact. A pitch deck should tell a story with a beginning (the problem), middle (the solution), and end (the outcome). Claude can restructure your ideas into this arc.

**Handling Objections Proactively**

The best proposals anticipate and address objections before the reader raises them:

• Give Claude the likely objections: "The client will probably push back on the timeline and the cost. Help me address both in the proposal without being defensive."
• Ask Claude to identify weak points: "What are the three strongest objections someone could make to this proposal?" Then work with Claude to address each one.
• Include a "Why now" section: Claude can help articulate the urgency without resorting to artificial pressure — focus on market timing, competitive dynamics, or cost-of-delay arguments.

sections.title: Adapting Tone and Style for Different Contexts
sections.content:
One of the most valuable skills in business writing is matching the tone to the context. A Slack message to your team should sound different from a board update. A congratulatory note should feel different from a project postmortem. Claude can shift between these registers, but only if you're explicit about what you want.

**The Business Writing Tone Spectrum**

Think of business tone on a spectrum from casual to formal, and from positive to critical. Most business communications fall into one of these zones:

• **Casual-Positive.** Team Slack messages, internal celebrations, informal check-ins. Claude instruction: "Write this like a friendly team message. Use contractions, keep it short, and make it feel personal rather than corporate."
• **Professional-Neutral.** Status updates, meeting recaps, process documentation. Claude instruction: "Professional and clear. No unnecessary warmth, no stiffness. Just clean, organized prose that conveys information efficiently."
• **Professional-Positive.** Client updates with good news, success stories, partnership communications. Claude instruction: "Warm and professional. Celebrate the achievement without overselling. Let the results speak for themselves."
• **Formal-Neutral.** Board reports, compliance documentation, legal communications. Claude instruction: "Formal register. No contractions, no casual language. Every claim should be precise and defensible. Err on the side of thoroughness over brevity."
• **Professional-Critical.** Difficult conversations, performance feedback, project escalations. Claude instruction: "Direct and respectful. State the issue clearly without softening it so much that the message gets lost. Use specific examples, not generalizations."

**Style Guides and Voice Consistency**

If your organization has a style guide or brand voice document, share it with Claude:

• Paste the key guidelines into your prompt: "Our brand voice is confident but not arrogant, helpful but not patronizing. We use active voice, avoid jargon, and always explain technical concepts in plain language."
• Provide examples of writing that matches your preferred style: "Here's a past email I wrote that has the right tone. Match this style for the new email."
• Specify vocabulary preferences: "We say 'team members,' not 'employees.' We say 'challenge,' not 'problem.' We never use 'circle back' or 'touch base.'"

**Context-Switching Between Audiences**

In a typical workday, you might write a Slack message to your team, an email to a client, and a summary for your VP — all about the same topic. Claude can help you write all three if you give it the appropriate context for each:

• Share the core information once, then ask Claude to adapt it for each audience: "Here's what happened with the product launch. Write three versions: (1) a casual Slack update for my engineering team, (2) a professional email to our client partner, and (3) a concise summary for my VP."
• Review each version for appropriateness. Claude occasionally borrows tone from one context and applies it to another — the Slack version might be too formal, or the VP summary might be too casual. A quick read catches these crossovers.

**Cultural Sensitivity in International Communication**

If you work with international colleagues or clients, ask Claude to adjust for cultural communication norms:

• "This email is going to our Japan team. Adjust the directness level — be more indirect in the request and include more context before making the ask."
• "Our German partners prefer very precise, structured communication. Remove any vague phrasing and organize the information in a logical hierarchy."
• These are generalizations, and Claude will note that. But as starting points for cross-cultural communication, they help you avoid the most common friction points.

sections.title: Create a Business Proposal with Claude
sections.content: Practice using Claude as a collaborative writing partner to develop a professional business proposal.

exercise.prompt:
You're a consultant preparing a proposal for a mid-sized retail company that wants to implement AI-powered customer service. They currently handle 500 support tickets per day with a team of 20 agents, average response time is 4 hours, and customer satisfaction score is 72%.

Write a series of 3 prompts you would send to Claude to develop this proposal, each building on the previous one:

1. **First prompt: Problem Statement and Solution Overview.** Write the prompt you'd use to get Claude to draft the opening section of the proposal. Include all five elements of a business writing prompt (role, audience, purpose, key points, constraints).

2. **Second prompt: Objection Handling.** Write a follow-up prompt that asks Claude to anticipate and address the client's likely concerns about implementing AI in customer service (job displacement fears, quality concerns, implementation complexity).

3. **Third prompt: Tone Refinement.** Write a final prompt that asks Claude to review and adjust the tone of the full proposal — making it confident and persuasive without being pushy, and technical enough to be credible without being inaccessible to a non-technical CEO.

For each prompt, briefly explain your reasoning: why you included certain elements and what you expect Claude to produce.
hints: In the first prompt, include the specific metrics (500 tickets/day, 4-hour response time, 72% CSAT) so Claude can reference real numbers rather than making generic claims
For objection handling, list the specific objections you want addressed — don't rely on Claude to guess what the client will worry about
For tone refinement, provide a positive example ('the proposal should sound like this') rather than only negative constraints ('don't be too salesy')
Consider the order of your prompts — starting with substance and ending with style is usually more efficient than the reverse
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should include: (1) A first prompt that includes all five elements — role (consultant), audience (mid-sized retail CEO or decision-maker), purpose (persuade to adopt AI customer service), key points (current metrics, proposed solution, expected improvements), and constraints (length, tone, format). The prompt should reference the specific data provided. (2) A second prompt that identifies at least three specific objections (job displacement, quality/accuracy, implementation complexity or cost) and asks Claude to address each with empathy and evidence rather than dismissal. (3) A third prompt that gives Claude specific tone guidance — not just 'make it better' but targeted adjustments like 'more confident in the ROI section' or 'softer language around the transition plan.' (4) Reasoning for each prompt that demonstrates understanding of the iterative writing process and why substance comes before style. Deduct points for prompts that lack the five elements, that don't use the provided metrics, or that give Claude too little direction on tone (e.g., just saying 'make it professional').

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Using Claude for business writing is about providing the right context, not the right words:

• **Include all five prompt elements.** Role, audience, purpose, key points, and constraints — every business writing prompt needs these. The more context Claude has about the situation, the more human and appropriate the output will be.

• **Use Claude as an editor, not just a drafter.** Some of the highest-value uses are refining existing writing: adjusting tone, cutting length, checking for sensitivity, and testing how the message reads from the recipient's perspective.

• **Match tone to context deliberately.** Business writing ranges from casual Slack updates to formal board communications, and the same information needs different treatment at each level. Be explicit about where on the spectrum you need Claude to write.

• **Build iteratively.** Start with substance (the facts and arguments), then refine structure (the flow and emphasis), then polish style (the tone and word choice). Trying to get all three right in a single prompt usually produces mediocre results on all three dimensions.

• **Maintain your voice.** Claude is a writing tool, not a writing replacement. Review every output to ensure it sounds like you, reflects your judgment, and makes the points you would make. The best AI-assisted writing is indistinguishable from your own — because it captures your thinking in cleaner prose.
